Coal-to-olefins has been said to reduce China's reliance on oil to keep the petchem industry running.

My very rudimentary way of explaining it:

The heart of the petrochemical industry is naphtha, which is produced from the CDU. You then use this to put into a steam cracker and produce ethylene, propylene and a bunch of other cracked products. These are all called "Olefins". These is then used into the downstream petchem derivatives industry to make all sorts of plastics.

There is another technology where you take COAL and make ethylene and propylene out of it. This is where the term Coal-to-olefins (CTO) is born. Essentially you bypass the steam cracker entirely. Presto! No oil needed to manufacture plastics!

What China then did with the freed up naphtha is to divert it into mogas blending instead to support the transportation demand. Tada! Oil demand reduced!

Not sure how structural this would be, but if the econs make sense and the government doesn't disapprove of CTO, why won't it be? China also brought online several steam crackers that takes ethane as feedstock. Yet another clever way to diversify out of naphtha.

Of course, the rest of Asia does not have this flexibility. So naphtha (and LPG) still would be required to keep the rest of their petchem units running.

Bearish Oil Positions Risk Reversal

Investors have become extremely bearish about the outlook for crude oil prices as the re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz has pushed the spot market into a temporary surplus.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of another 35 million barrels in the main ICE Brent futures and options contract over the seven days ending on June 30.

Funds have sold Brent in twelve of the last thirteen weeks, reducing their position by a total of 374 million barrels since March 31 ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

The last 24 hours in the Strait of Hormuz are a doozy.

At the start of the clip, you will see many of the vessels that were going through the Oman route take the Iran route instead.

Now there's something I should probably point out. In the last week or so, US escorts through the Oman lane typically saw VLCCs transit out; that was not the case this time. In addition, US transits typically show a cluster of ships moving together, which was not the case here either.

Basically, I'm just saying it doesn't look like a typical US escort. But it was also interesting that some of the vessels were able to leave right away via the Iran route, while others turned back.

I'm not entirely sure what's going on.

Visible AIS data-wise, there were no other escorts that took place. Inbound VLCCs were zero today for the first time since the MOU was signed. I will need to check the satellite data tomorrow, but I'm sure some went in; we just don't know right now.

What's clear, however, is that the Oman route will be heavily contested in the coming days. Any assumption of normality in the Strait of Hormuz is wishful thinking.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 fell -1.6% on Thursday, while the semiconductor index, $SOX, dropped -5.4%.

Memory stocks led the decline, posting their biggest 2-day drop in at least 12 years, falling more than -18%, according to Goldman Sachs.

The selloff was triggered by a report that Anthropic is in talks with Samsung to manufacture a custom AI chip, reviving concerns over memory pricing and the sustainability of AI infrastructure spending.

Meanwhile, defensive sectors significantly outperformed, with consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare stocks all advancing as investors rotated out of technology.

Oil extended its decline as flows through the Strait of Hormuz surged to 14 million barrels per day on July 1, pushing front-month Brent down to ~$70, from $112 in mid-May.

AI Leads US Job Cuts for Record 4th Month as Tech Claims 31% of H1 Layoffs | Mark Rutherford, Techtimes

Artificial intelligence has become the leading stated reason for US job cuts for four consecutive months — a streak with no precedent in outplacement data — and the technology sector has absorbed nearly a third of all US layoff announcements in the first half of 2026, according to a report released Wednesday by outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas. Between January and June, US tech employers announced 139,156 job cuts — an 83% surge from the 76,214 recorded in the same period of 2025 — while AI was explicitly cited in 101,743 of all US layoff announcements this year, representing approximately 23% of every cut tracked by Challenger, Gray & Christmas across the entire economy.

The back-to-back-to-back-to-back months in which AI ranked as the leading stated reason for layoffs marks a structural shift that Challenger's own data — which began tracking AI as a distinct cut reason in 2023 — has not recorded before. "Tech remains the epicenter of this year's cuts," said Andy Challenger, chief revenue officer at the Chicago-based firm. "AI is the dominant force as companies are restructuring around it, automating roles, and reallocating budgets toward new capabilities. The sector is being reshaped in real time."

But the firms driving that streak are, in almost every case, the same firms posting their strongest-ever financial results — a paradox that has drawn escalating scrutiny from economists, labor researchers, and the workers on the receiving end of the cuts.

What Tech's 31% Share of US Layoffs Actually Means for Workers

The headline June numbers offered some surface-level relief. US employers across all sectors announced 45,849 job cuts during the month — the lowest monthly total since December 2025, down 53% from May's 97,006. Tech specifically saw 15,503 cuts in June, down sharply from May's 38,242, which had been the sector's highest single-month total since August 2024.

Challenger attributed the June moderation to seasonal patterns rather than any underlying change in direction. "The pace of layoffs cooled considerably in June, similar to plans last June, and as is typical for summer months," he said. "That said, the cuts we are seeing remain concentrated in technology, and artificial intelligence continues to reshape how companies think about headcount."

The full first-half picture reinforces that reading. Total US layoff announcements reached 443,604 through June — down 40% from the government-distorted 744,308 recorded in H1 2025, when mass federal workforce reductions drove that period's total to historic highs. Adjusting for that distortion, 2026 is running roughly in line with 2024. What has fundamentally changed is where the cuts are falling: tech's share has ballooned to nearly a third of the remaining total, a concentration last seen during the post-pandemic correction of 2022-2023. In the second quarter alone, employers announced 226,242 cuts, up 4% from Q1's 217,362 — signaling that the year's structural trend is not abating even as monthly figures bounce seasonally.

Profitable Companies Cut While Committing Record Capex

The Challenger data crystallizes what analysts have called the defining paradox of 2026's technology labor market: the same companies eliminating tens of thousands of roles are simultaneously committing to the largest capital investment cycle in the industry's history.

Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta have collectively guided 2026 capital expenditure to an estimated $700 billion — nearly double their combined 2025 actual spend — with the bulk directed at AI data centers, custom silicon, and inference infrastructure. Meta reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $56.3 billion, up 33% year over year, then executed 8,000 layoffs in May while raising its 2026 capex guidance to $115-145 billion. Google Cloud revenue grew 63% year over year in Q1 2026. Microsoft reported revenue of $82.9 billion and operating income of $38.4 billion in its fiscal third quarter of 2026, up 18% and 20% year over year respectively.

The financial logic driving simultaneous cuts and capex is visible in corporate filings. Meta's $115-145 billion infrastructure spending envelope dwarfs its human payroll budget — meaning the layoffs function more as a financing mechanism than as a direct cost-saving exercise. Cisco provides the year's starkest illustration: on May 14, the San Jose networking company notified roughly 4,000 workers of their elimination on the same day it reported record quarterly revenue of $15.8 billion, a 12% year-on-year gain. CFO Mark Patterson told analysts that the restructuring was "not a savings-driven" exercise but a rapid reallocation toward silicon, optics, security, and AI.

"Regardless of whether individual jobs are being replaced by AI, the money for those roles is," Challenger said in an earlier report this year.

Is AI Washing Inflating the Numbers? What Economists Say

The Challenger methodology records the reason companies themselves report for their cuts — it does not independently verify causation. That distinction has generated sustained scrutiny from economists and, unusually, from some of the technology industry's own leaders.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged the gap in February and again in March, saying: "Almost every company that does layoffs is blaming AI, whether or not it really is about AI." His framing — which he labeled "AI washing" — captures a dynamic Deutsche Bank analysts had predicted in January: "AI redundancy washing will be a significant feature of 2026."

The critics are specific about the mechanism. Marc Andreessen argued that companies are using AI as a "silver bullet excuse" for correcting pandemic-era overhiring — which he estimated left many large tech firms overstaffed by 25% to 75%. Wharton management professor Peter Cappelli put the same point more bluntly: "The headline is, 'It's because of AI,' but if you read what they actually say, they say, 'We expect that AI will cover this work.' Hadn't done it. They're just hoping." Oxford Economics concluded in January that firms "don't appear to be replacing workers with AI on a significant scale."

Even Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang called the practice "lazy," saying it did not make business sense that companies were replacing workers at the current scale through AI systems. Tim Sweeney, CEO of Epic Games, broke with the prevailing corporate script explicitly when announcing more than 1,000 cuts: "Since it's a thing now, I should note that the layoffs aren't related to AI."

EY-Parthenon chief economist Greg Daco offered a more measured reading. "We are seeing a lot of layoff announcements that are supposedly related to greater use of AI," he said. "They are aimed at cutting down on labor expenses, while AI investment is growing very rapidly, but I'm not entirely sure this is a replacement situation where talent is being one-for-one substituted."

What is not in dispute is the budget mechanism. Challenger's Andy Challenger described it plainly: companies are "shifting budgets toward AI investments at the expense of jobs." Not direct replacement — budget reallocation. The distinction matters for workers who are told their roles were automated but whose replacement is a GPU cluster, not a chatbot doing their job.

A Gartner survey of 350 executives at companies with revenue above $1 billion — all of them already deploying AI — found that roughly 80% had reduced headcount. But Gartner analyst Helen Poitevin identified a critical flaw in the premise: "Workforce reductions may create budget room, but they do not create return. Organizations that improve ROI are not those that eliminate the need for people, but those that amplify them." The survey found no meaningful difference in financial returns between companies making the deepest cuts and those cutting the least.

What Happens When AI Absorbs Junior Developer Work

Whatever the precise attribution, the structural effects are measurable and concentrated in a specific cohort. Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index found that employment for software developers between the ages of 22 and 25 fell nearly 20% from 2024 levels — even as headcount for developers over 26 continued to grow.

The mechanism is architectural. AI tools — code-generation assistants, scripted testing platforms, automated bug-fix pipelines — now perform the boilerplate and routine coding that junior roles were historically built around. Senior engineers, equipped with those tools, can handle the same throughput without a junior team beneath them. The junior role is not being automated directly; it is being compressed out of existence because the work that defined it no longer requires a separate human to execute.

This creates a consequence the data does not yet show but that the talent pipeline structure makes likely: if companies stop hiring junior engineers at scale, they stop producing mid-level and senior engineers at scale. The experience ladder — entry-level to mid-level to principal — runs through the roles that are disappearing. One-third of surveyed organizations in the Stanford report anticipated workforce reductions due to AI in the coming year, with expected cuts highest in software engineering, service operations, and supply chain.

The human cost is already accumulating. A Goldman Sachs analysis published in April 2026 estimated that AI is eliminating roughly 25,000 US jobs per month while creating approximately 9,000 new ones — a net monthly reduction of 16,000. Reemployment timelines for laid-off tech workers are stretching, reflecting both the volume of displaced workers entering the market and a widening mismatch between eliminated roles and available positions.

Ken Matos, an organizational psychologist and director of insights at hiring platform HiBob, expects hiring to rebound after the current investment cycle matures. "Right now, companies are moving labor dollars into tech investment," he said. "Hopefully, that moves back into labor dollars once the technology is set up." But he was direct about who would benefit: "The people who get laid off don't necessarily get the next set of jobs, because the roles are different."

Does the AI Layoff Explanation Hold Up Without a Law to Test It?

One reason the AI washing debate cannot be resolved from the outside is structural: no federal law currently requires employers to disclose whether AI played a material role in a mass layoff.

The federal WARN Act — enacted in 1988, requiring 60 days' advance notice for mass layoffs at companies with 100 or more employees — mandates notice but imposes no obligation to state a reason. A company can publicly attribute thousands of layoffs to AI, and affected workers have no legal right to verify or contest that explanation.

A pending federal bill, the AI Workforce PREPARE Act, would amend the WARN Act to require employers to disclose when AI was a "substantial factor" in a mass layoff, name the AI systems used, estimate the percentage of job losses attributable to them, and describe any retraining steps taken before cuts were made. The bill has not passed.

At the state level, California has moved furthest and fastest. In May, Governor Gavin Newsom signed Executive Order N-6-26, directing the California Labor and Workforce Development Agency to undertake a 180-day review of potential revisions to the state's Cal-WARN Act — including shorter trigger thresholds and new AI-specific disclosure requirements. California also launched what state officials described as the nation's first public dashboard tracking potential AI-related job displacement, using unemployment insurance data to identify early indicators by occupation. Initial data from the tracker showed no evidence of rising statewide unemployment claims in AI-exposed occupations overall, though it did identify impacts on workers in high-exposure positions.

California's SB 951 — which would require 90 days' advance written notice before AI-driven layoffs affecting 25 or more workers — has stalled in the state Assembly. A federal companion bill, the No Robot Bosses Act, which would require human oversight when AI tools are used in employment decisions, remains pending in Congress. Similar disclosure bills have been introduced in at least seven states this legislative session, but none have become law. Colorado's original AI governance law, which had been scheduled to take effect June 30, 2026, was replaced by revised legislation before it took effect; the replacement narrows the state's framework to a disclosure-only model and takes effect January 1, 2027, with enforcement left to the state Attorney General and no private right of action.

RAISE US and the Retraining Gap

On June 25 — eight days before this report — former Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo and former Indiana Governor Eric Holcomb launched RAISE US, a nonpartisan nonprofit backed by Amazon, Anthropic, Microsoft, and the OpenAI Foundation, along with more than two dozen corporate and philanthropic partners. The coalition has raised more than $500 million toward a $1 billion multi-year goal, with initial pilot programs in Arkansas, Connecticut, Maryland, and Utah.

The initiative carries an embedded irony that Raimondo acknowledged directly: the companies building the technology that employers cite when cutting those jobs are writing the first checks to retrain the workers displaced by those cuts. "America has a technology strategy for leading the global AI competition," she said at the launch. "It does not yet have a people strategy, and we cannot lead without one."

Raimondo herself called past federal retraining efforts "ineffective" — a candid assessment that doubles as the central challenge RAISE US must answer. The historical record is not reassuring. A 2025 Brookings Institution analysis reviewed six decades of US federal retraining programs and found that randomized controlled trials showed no statistically significant improvement in employment or earnings for participants. An arXiv study published in May 2026 analyzed more than 23 million participation records from the federal Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act between 2017 and 2023, finding that retraining rarely moved workers into less automation-exposed jobs.

The RAISE US advisory board draws on serious labor economists — MIT's David Autor, Harvard's Raj Chetty, Erik Brynjolfsson — and includes AFL-CIO President Liz Shuler, a deliberate signal of labor-table inclusion. Whether $500 million in privately funded pilots can produce outcomes that six decades of federal programs did not is the question the next three years will answer.

What the Next Four Years Will Reveal

Economists broadly caution against projecting the current trend forward without accounting for the investment cycle it is embedded in. The $700 billion committed by the four largest hyperscalers in 2026 must begin generating sufficient revenue to justify its own depreciation burden — a reckoning that most forecasters place in the 2027-2030 window. If AI-driven productivity gains materialize at the scale companies are betting on, the revenue base expands and some displaced roles eventually return in reconfigured form. If they do not, the workforce reduction will have preceded the productivity gain, leaving both workers and investors worse off.

What is not speculative is the concentration of capital involved. The four hyperscalers are now committing more to data centers annually than ExxonMobil, Chevron, Shell, and BP combined spend on oil and gas exploration. BCG has projected that up to 15% of US jobs could face elimination over the next five years if AI adoption broadens beyond the technology sector — though that scenario, cited in an earlier report, remains unconfirmed at scale and is contested by economists including Daron Acemoğlu, who argues humans will remain necessary and complementary to AI rather than replaced by it.

"Right now, companies are moving labor dollars into tech investment," Matos said. "Hopefully, that moves back into labor dollars once the technology is set up."

For any worker in an AI-exposed role, the question is whether the transition window — during which payroll budgets have shifted to GPU procurement but AI-generated productivity revenue has not yet materialized — is short enough to survive. The H1 2026 Challenger data suggests the window is open and widening. Whether RAISE US, stalled disclosure bills, or eventually a labor market rebound closes it before the next generation of engineers fails to enter the pipeline is what 2026 through 2030 will determine. For workers deciding now whether to stay in an AI-exposed role, upskill toward AI-adjacent work, or wait for the legislative backstop that has not yet arrived, the H1 data offers a clear signal: the restructuring is accelerating, the policy response is thin, and the companies funding the only retraining initiative on the table are the same ones executing the cuts.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are companies really cutting jobs because of AI, or is that a cover story?

Both, according to named economists and company executives. Outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas records whatever reason companies themselves report — no independent verification of causation is required. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged in public remarks that some AI attribution is genuine displacement while some is what he called "AI washing": citing AI for cuts the company would have made anyway. Oxford Economics concluded in January 2026 that firms "don't appear to be replacing workers with AI on a significant scale." The clearest documented pattern is budget reallocation: payroll savings from workforce reductions are being redirected to AI infrastructure investment. Challenger's own framing captures this directly — "the money for those roles is" being replaced by AI spending, whether or not the specific task was automated.

Why are profitable companies cutting jobs while spending hundreds of billions on AI?

The financial logic is a capital reallocation story rather than a cost-cutting story. Meta, for example, reported $56.3 billion in first-quarter 2026 revenue — up 33% year over year — while simultaneously executing layoffs and guiding its 2026 capital expenditure to $115-145 billion, mostly for AI infrastructure. The workforce reduction frees up operating cash that is then redirected to infrastructure whose depreciation costs will run for years. Cisco cut 4,000 workers on the day it reported record quarterly revenue of $15.8 billion; its CFO called the restructuring "not savings-driven." A Gartner survey of 350 enterprises found that companies making the deepest cuts showed no improvement in financial returns over those cutting the least — suggesting the layoffs are funding a bet on future AI returns that has not yet materialized.

What federal law protects workers from AI-attributed layoffs, and what are its gaps?

The federal WARN Act (1988) requires 60 days' advance notice before mass layoffs at companies with 100 or more employees — but it does not require employers to state any reason for the layoffs. A company can publicly cite AI as the cause and workers have no legal right to verify or contest that explanation. The pending AI Workforce PREPARE Act would amend WARN to require AI-reason disclosure, including naming the specific systems used and estimating the percentage of cuts attributable to them — but the bill has not passed. California's SB 951, which would require 90 days' notice for AI-driven layoffs, is stalled in the state Assembly. As of now, the federal disclosure gap means that Challenger's AI-cited figures rest entirely on voluntary, unverified corporate self-reporting.

If AI is eliminating junior developer roles, what happens to the next generation of senior engineers?

This is the structural consequence the current numbers do not yet show but the talent pipeline makes likely. Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index documented a nearly 20% drop in employment for software developers ages 22-25, while headcount for older developers grew. The mechanism is task compression: AI code-generation and testing tools let senior engineers absorb the boilerplate work that junior roles were built around, so companies stop hiring juniors rather than replacing them directly. The consequence is a pipeline gap: if organizations stop training junior engineers at scale in 2026, the cohort that would have become mid-level and senior engineers in 2028-2030 simply does not exist. Gartner forecasts that half of companies that attributed headcount reductions to AI will rehire staff in similar functions under different titles by 2027 — but whether those rehires will come from a junior pipeline that no longer exists is the open question that the H1 2026 data cannot yet answer.

押注霍尔木兹航运恢复，机构预测布油年底最低或跌至60美元

财联社7月4日讯（编辑 赵昊）随着全球石油市场有望重新转向供过于求，一些华尔街策略师开始认为布油价格到今年年底可能跌至每桶60美元，这一水平相当于较中东战事期间的峰值腰斩。

花旗集团分析师在周五的一份报告中表示，尽管局势可能出现短暂波动，但美伊双方都有充分理由维持6月中旬签署的谅解备忘录，石油供应增加将在今年下半年对油价形成下行压力。

花旗建议交易员在今夏油价反弹时逢高卖出，并预测到今年年底，布伦特原油价格将在每桶60至65美元之间。

美伊签署谅解备忘录至今已有两周，但双方围绕备忘录的具体条款和执行依然争论不休

对此，花旗分析师写道，“我们预计谅解备忘录将得到维持，并不是因为双方突然建立了信任，而是因为打破协议对双方都缺乏足够的激励。”

“更广泛的政治背景表明，局势将趋于稳定并最终达成协议，而不是重新走向对抗。”

他们还指出，石油市场的供需基本面仍然“结构性疲软”，“如果产油国决定加快已启动的增产计划，市场供需平衡可能迅速转向明显过剩”。

布伦特原油自2月底以来大幅上涨，随着霍尔木兹海峡被迫关闭航运后进一步飙升。4月30日，布油一度突破每桶126美元，创下2022年以来最高水平。

但随着中东冲突缓和，以及6月美伊签署谅解备忘录后，油价持续回落。截至发稿，布伦特原油报每桶72.13美元。

需要指出的是，花旗预测的油价明显低于市场普遍预期。目前，华尔街对2026年底布油价格的预测中值为78美元，而布油2027年1月交割合约报73美元附近。

上月，高盛将其年底布油价格预测下调至每桶80美元。该行近期报告指出，海湾地区石油运输迅速恢复，80美元的预测基于霍尔木兹海峡的航运流量将在7月底前恢复至战前水平。

高盛也表示，在更乐观的供应正常化情景下，布伦特原油价格到2026年底可能降至每桶60美元左右。

不过，这一预测的前提包括：海湾地区石油出口在“7月初”恢复至战前水平、全球原油日产量比预期高出100万桶以上，以及全球需求弱于预期。

花旗分析师称，目前通过霍尔木兹海峡的原油运输量已回升至每日700万桶。与之相比，冲突爆发前约为每日1500万桶。

他们还指出，实际运输量可能高于官方统计数据，因为出于安全考虑，许多油轮关闭了船舶应答器，因此未被完整纳入统计。

Gold Faces Resistance While Crude Oil Builds a Base: What Traders Should Watch Next

Commodity markets are entering an interesting phase, with gold and silver approaching key resistance levels, while crude oil appears to be forming a strong base after a prolonged decline. This combination presents both opportunities and risks for traders. Understanding price action, support and resistance, and trend confirmation is essential before taking fresh positions.

Gold has staged a healthy recovery after recent weakness, supported by safe-haven demand and improving investor sentiment. However, the broader technical trend remains cautious as the metal approaches major resistance zones. Historically, strong resistance levels often trigger profit booking, especially after a sharp rally. Unless gold manages to break above these levels with convincing volume, traders should avoid chasing higher prices. Instead, booking partial profits and waiting for a confirmed breakout may prove to be a smarter strategy.

Silver has outperformed gold in recent sessions, reflecting stronger industrial demand and improved market sentiment. However, silver is also nearing an important resistance zone. A decisive breakout could fuel another leg higher, but failure to cross resistance may invite renewed selling pressure. Traders should closely monitor volume and price behaviour before initiating fresh long positions.

Crude oil, on the other hand, is showing signs of stabilization after an extended downtrend. Brent crude has found support near important technical levels, indicating that sellers may be losing momentum. While the broader trend has not yet turned fully bullish, the formation of a base increases the probability of a corrective rally. If crude oil sustains above its support zone, buyers could gradually regain control and push prices toward higher resistance levels.

Several macroeconomic factors will continue to influence commodity prices, including US Federal Reserve policy, inflation data, global economic growth, geopolitical developments, OPEC+ production decisions, and currency movements. These events can create sharp price swings, making disciplined risk management more important than ever.

For traders, the ideal approach is to follow confirmation rather than speculation. Buying only after confirmed breakouts, respecting stop-loss levels, and maintaining a favorable risk-reward ratio can significantly improve long-term trading performance. Emotional trading during volatile commodity markets often leads to avoidable losses.

Conclusion

Gold and silver remain fundamentally strong but face immediate technical hurdles, while crude oil appears to be building a foundation for a potential recovery. Rather than predicting the market, traders should focus on price confirmation, disciplined execution, and effective risk management. In commodities, patience and preparation often outperform aggressive trading decisions.

250 YEARS LATER, THE FOUNDERS FOREIGN POLICY STILL GUIDES AMERICA

Today, July 4, 2026, the United States of America turns 250 years old.

Two and a half centuries ago, fifty-six men signed the Declaration of Independence in Philadelphia, pledging their lives, their fortunes, and their sacred honor to an idea the world had never seen tried at scale: a free republic, governed by its own people, beholden to no king.

Anniversaries like this one usually produce fireworks and speeches.  

This article proposes something different: a look at what the Founders actually believed about America's place in the world, because their answer is far more relevant to 2026 than most people imagine.

Consider what happened just weeks ago.

Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil passes.  

Tehran demanded that the world pay the price of its ambitions.  

President Trump responded with decisive naval force, reopened the strait, and refused to reward the regime's aggression with concessions of any kind.

Here is what few commentators noticed: America has faced this exact situation before.  Different ocean.  Different century.  Different pirates.  Same principle.

In 1801, President Thomas Jefferson sent the young United States Navy and the Marines across the Atlantic to crush the Barbary pirates of North Africa, who had spent years seizing American merchant ships and demanding tribute for safe passage.

The parallels are not a coincidence.  

They are evidence of something this article intends to demonstrate: the Founders left behind a foreign policy compass that still points true, 250 years later.

The sentence everyone skips: "The great rule of conduct"

Ask most educated Americans what George Washington said about foreign policy, and they will quote his Farewell Address of 1796: "Steer clear of permanent alliances with any portion of the foreign world."

Almost nobody quotes the sentence that comes before it.

"The great rule of conduct for us in regard to foreign nations is in extending our commercial relations, to have with them as little political connection as possible."

Read that carefully.  

Washington's great rule, his own words, begins with EXTENDING commercial relations.  Engagement with the world comes first.  The limitation on political connection comes second.

We mention this other quote because this single sentence demolishes the most common misconception about the Founders: that they were isolationists who wanted America to hide behind its oceans.  

They were the opposite.  

They were global traders who guarded their political sovereignty jealously.  Commerce with everyone.  Political entanglement with no one.

Washington went further.  

He warned against "permanent, inveterate antipathies against particular nations, and passionate attachments for others," because a nation driven by emotional attachments becomes "in some degree a slave" to them.  

National interest, not sentiment, should guide the republic.

And in one of the most beautiful phrases of the entire address, he looked forward to the day when America would be strong enough that it "may choose peace or war, as our interest, guided by justice, shall counsel."

Interest, guided by justice.  Strength, restrained by morality.  That is the Founders foreign policy in five words.

A philosophy is born, then tested

Washington published his Farewell Address in September 1796.  

It would have remained a beautiful theory if history had not immediately put it through three brutal tests, all within the lifetime of the men who wrote it.

The first test came almost instantly.

Test one: the Quasi-War with France (1798-1800).  France, angry that America refused to join its war against Britain, began seizing American merchant ships.  

In 1796 and 1797 alone, French raiders captured some 316 American vessels.  

The pressure to abandon neutrality and pick a side in Europe's war was enormous.  

President John Adams refused to do either. Instead, he built a navy, including the famous frigates USS Constitution, USS United States, and USS Constellation. 

That navy fought an undeclared war at sea strictly to defend American shipping. The result: peace with France, without a single permanent alliance. 

The principle tested: America would defend its commerce with force, but it would not be dragged into Europe's wars.  Neutrality survived its first trial by fire.

Test two: the First Barbary War (1801-1805).  For years, the Muslim states of the North African coast, Tripoli, Algiers, Tunis, and Morocco, had operated a protection racket on the Mediterranean.  

Pay tribute, or your ships get seized and your sailors get enslaved.  

Under Washington and Adams, America paid.  

By 1797, the United States had paid out 1,250,000 dollars in tribute, a staggering one-fifth of the entire federal budget at the time.  

The pirates took the money and kept seizing ships anyway.

When Jefferson became president in 1801, the ruler of Tripoli demanded even more tribute from the new administration.  

Jefferson had reached his enough-is-enough moment.  He refused to pay, and Tripoli declared war by chopping down the flagpole of the American consulate.

Jefferson, the small-government philosopher, the man who feared standing armies, sent the Navy and the Marines four thousand miles from home to bombard the pirate fortresses and blockade their harbors.  

Lieutenant Stephen Decatur's night raid to burn the captured frigate USS Philadelphia in Tripoli harbor was called "the most daring act of the age" by British Admiral Lord Nelson himself.  

The Marines' march on the shores of Tripoli lives forever in the opening line of the Marines' Hymn.

The national slogan of that era says everything: "Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute."

The principle tested: freedom of navigation and commerce would be defended with force, anywhere on earth, no matter the distance.  

Note what the war was NOT about: Jefferson did not try to change Tripoli's government, occupy its territory, or transform its society.  

He restored free passage for American ships and went home.

Test three: the War of 1812 (1812-1815).  Britain, locked in its death struggle with Napoleon, refused to treat America as a truly sovereign nation.  

The Royal Navy stopped American ships at sea and seized American sailors, forcing them to serve on British warships.  

This practice was called impressment: the forced recruitment of American citizens on the theory that anyone born under the British crown remained a subject of the British crown forever, no matter what any Declaration of Independence said.  

Thousands of American sailors were taken this way.

President James Madison, the father of the Constitution, led the nation into war to answer a simple question: was American sovereignty real, or was it negotiable?  

The war was brutal.  

The British burned Washington, including the White House.  But America survived, Andrew Jackson shattered the British at New Orleans, and no European power ever again treated American citizenship as a technicality.

And in 1815, in a final echo, Commodore Decatur returned to the Mediterranean and ended the Barbary tribute system forever in a war that lasted all of three days.

Then, in 1823, came the culmination.  

President James Monroe declared that the Western Hemisphere was closed to any new European colonization, a warning aimed not only at Britain, but equally at France, Spain, and Russia, all of whom had territorial ambitions in the Americas.

The Monroe Doctrine transformed the Founders' defensive principles into a regional strategy: this half of the world is under American protection.

Look at the sequence as a whole.  1796: the philosophy is articulated.  1798: it is tested.  1801: it is applied offensively.  1812: it is defended at existential cost.  1823: it is expanded into a doctrine.  

This was never theory.  It was a working system, proven under fire, within a single generation.

Washington, Jefferson, Monroe: three stages of one strategy

The chronology above reveals a framework that explains early American foreign policy better than any textbook label.

Washington secured INDEPENDENCE.  

America would choose its own foreign policy, serve its own interests, and be dragged into no one's wars.  The Farewell Address is the founding charter of American strategic freedom.

Jefferson secured COMMERCIAL EXPANSION.  

America would trade with the entire world, and it would defend its right to navigate the seas with force whenever necessary, at any distance.  The Barbary Wars established that American commerce sails under American protection.

Monroe secured REGIONAL STRATEGIC LEADERSHIP.  

The Western Hemisphere would be shielded from outside domination.  America's security begins with a secure neighborhood.

Independence.  Commerce.  Regional leadership. 

Each stage builds on the one before it.  

A nation must first be free to act, then prosperous enough to matter, then strong enough to lead.  

It is not merely a chronology.  It is an evolution of strategic thought, and it remains the deep structure of American foreign policy to this day.

The Monroe Doctrine in particular deserves a modern reading.  

In 1823, the threat was European empires planting flags in the Americas.  

In 2026, the threat is economic colonization: Chinese state companies buying ports from Peru to Panama, Belt and Road debt traps across the region, and Beijing converting infrastructure into influence one loan at a time.  

The flags changed.  The game did not.  

A twenty-first century Monroe Doctrine watches America's backyard not for European frigates but for foreign creditors with geopolitical ambitions.

Hamilton: the Founder who sounds like he posts on X

One more Founder deserves a seat at this table, and he is the one most people associate with banking rather than foreign policy.

Alexander Hamilton argued, in his Report on Manufactures of 1791 and throughout his career, four propositions that sound remarkably like a 2026 policy platform.  

Economic strength IS national security.  Domestic manufacturing guarantees independence in war and peace.  Sound public credit is what funds military power when the crisis comes.  And a navy exists, above all, to protect commerce.

Hamilton did not believe a nation could be secure while depending on rivals for the goods it needs to survive.  

He wanted America to make things, build things, and owe its prosperity to no foreign power's goodwill.

Now listen to the modern debate about tariffs, reshoring critical industries, semiconductor plants, energy dominance, and the defense industrial base.  

The vocabulary has changed.  The argument has not.

This is worth stating plainly, because it corrects a second great misconception.  

The Founders were not free-trade absolutists, and they were not isolationists.  They were economic nationalists who traded globally from a position of strength.  

Hamilton subsidized American industry.  

Jefferson embargoed America's enemies.  

Washington demanded commercial treaties on equal terms and warned against granting any nation "exclusive favors or preferences."  

They wanted maximum commerce and maximum independence, and they saw no contradiction between the two, because there is none.

What the Founders opposed, in their own words

If the Founders were not isolationists, what exactly were they against?  

The answer is precise: permanent political alliances.  And their reasons were not abstract.

Why, they asked, should a farm boy from Virginia die in a quarrel between European kings?  

Europe, Washington wrote, "has a set of primary interests which to us have none, or a very remote relation."  Her endless controversies were "essentially foreign to our concerns."

Why should a young republic bury itself in debt to defend old empires?  

The Founders had just fought a war financed by borrowing, and they knew that war debt means taxes, and taxes mean a heavier state pressing on a free people.  

Prolonged foreign wars, many of them feared, would also require large standing armies, and standing armies had been the tool of tyrants throughout history.

And why should America trust that Europe's powers would prove loyal allies when the bill came due?  

The Founders had watched European monarchies switch sides, betray treaties, and abandon partners for a hundred years.  

They had no illusions that gratitude governs nations.  

Washington's answer was structural, not emotional: keep a strong defense at home, and "we may safely trust to temporary alliances for extraordinary emergencies."

Temporary alliances.  For extraordinary emergencies.  Not permanent ones, for ordinary politics.

The Founders' logic was not fear of the world.  It was a cold accounting of what wars cost, who pays, and who bleeds.  

Any modern reader can decide for themselves which of today's permanent arrangements would pass Washington's test, and which would not.

From Barbary tribute to Tehran's pallets: the bribe that never works

History does not repeat itself, but it rhymes with embarrassing precision.

Before Jefferson's Eureka moment, America's policy toward the Barbary states was appeasement by installment plan.  

Pay the tribute, ransom the hostages, hope the pirates stay bought.  

They never stayed bought.  

The demands always grew.  One-fifth of the federal budget went out the door, and American sailors still rotted in North African dungeons.

Two centuries later, Washington tried the same failed policy with a different set of ayatollahs.

In January 2016, the Obama administration flew 400 million dollars in cash, on wooden pallets, in an unmarked plane, to Tehran, the same day Iran released four American prisoners.  

It was the first installment of a 1,700 million dollar settlement.  By May of that year, Iran's Central Bank had transferred the money to the Iranian military.  

The Biden administration later unfroze an additional 6,000 million dollars in Iranian funds.

And what did the tribute buy?  

Iran took the money and kept building its nuclear program, kept arming Hezbollah, the Houthis, and its militias across the Middle East, and kept threatening the world's shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

Exactly as the Barbary pirates took the money and kept seizing ships.

Jefferson's conclusion in 1801 was that force is cheaper than tribute, because tribute never ends and force does.  

Trump reached the same conclusion with Iran's nuclear threat in 2026.  

It took a naval campaign, not another pallet of cash, to reopen Hormuz and bring Tehran to a memorandum of understanding.

The lesson survived 225 years because it is simply true: paying aggressors buys you more aggression.

Principles endure, policies adapt

Here we arrive at the philosophical heart of the argument.

Principles are answers to permanent questions.  What is government for?  What is worth fighting for?  Who decides a nation's course?  

Policies are answers to temporary questions.  Which enemy?  Which weapon?  Which ocean?

The Founders answered the permanent questions so well that their answers still work.  The temporary answers have changed completely, as they must.

The wooden frigate became the carrier strike group, and then the Air Force, the Space Force, and Cyber Command.  

Freedom of navigation on the Barbary coast became freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz and the South China Sea.  

The common defense of harbors and coastlines became the defense of satellites, networks, and power grids, because the Founders believed government exists in great part to provide the common defense, and the common defense today includes cyberspace.

The enemies changed too.  

America no longer worries about wars with Britain or France; today's great-power rival is Communist China.  

The Barbary corsairs are gone; their modern heirs are Islamic terrorists and a Tehran regime that treats a global waterway as its personal tollbooth.  

There is no point in sugarcoating who the pirates of our age are.

Set the two eras side by side and the continuity is striking.  

Protect commerce then meant frigates against corsairs; now it means keeping the sea lanes open, including Hormuz.  

Protect sovereignty then meant resisting impressment; now it means resisting foreign coercion and cyber attacks.  

A strong navy then; a strong Navy, Air Force, Space Force, and Cyber Command now.  

Avoid permanent entanglements then; be deeply cautious about open-ended military commitments now.  

Trade broadly then; trade globally under secure conditions now.

The tools evolve.  The objectives remain recognizable.

One more dimension of the Founders' worldview deserves to be named, because the modern retelling usually omits it.  

These men reasoned within a mostly Christian moral framework, and it was not decoration.  Many of their families had crossed an ocean fleeing religious persecution.  

They believed in just war as a last resort, in honest dealings between nations, and in a providence that had given this continent a different destiny than Europe's endless cycle of dynastic bloodshed.  

Washington himself wrote it into the Farewell Address: "Observe good faith and justice towards all nations; cultivate peace and harmony with all.  Religion and morality enjoin this conduct."

Scripture teaches, in Proverbs 22:7, that "the borrower is slave to the lender."  

The Founders applied that wisdom to nations as much as to men: a republic buried in war debt, or dependent on foreign powers for its survival, is not fully free, whatever its constitution says.  

Their foreign policy of strength, solvency, and independence was, at bottom, an application of biblical prudence to statecraft.

And this is why their vision was not merely national selfishness dressed in fine language.  

Most nations in history pursued naked interest.  The Founders pursued interest "guided by justice," in Washington's own phrase, because they genuinely believed liberty was a sacred trust.  

That conviction is what made America different, and it is what still separates America First from mere cynicism.

Answering the objections

An honest thesis anticipates its critics.  There are three serious objections to the argument of this article, and each deserves a straight answer.

Objection one: the Founders led a small coastal republic, not a global superpower.  True.  But principles scale.  

Monroe extended the same principles from thirteen states to an entire hemisphere within one generation.  The question was never the size of the nation; it was the logic of its conduct.

Objection two: the Founders could not have imagined nuclear weapons, cyberspace, or globalized supply chains.  

Also true, and it does not matter, because they did not write operating manuals.  They wrote principles.  A compass does not need to know the terrain in advance to point north.

Objection three: some modern alliances arose in circumstances the 1790s never faced, and some have served America well.  

Fair enough.  The Founders themselves allowed for "temporary alliances for extraordinary emergencies."  

Their test was never "no cooperation, ever."  

Their test was: does the arrangement serve American interests, or has America ended up serving it?  

Every alliance, every commitment, every treaty should have to answer that question, out loud, on a regular basis.  Arrangements that pass the test continue.  

Arrangements that cannot even face the question have answered it already.

To be clear about what this article does not claim. We are not claiming that the Founders invented America First. No serious historian would say that, and neither do we.

The claim is stronger for being precise.  

America First is the modern application of the Founders' principles, updated to today's geopolitical realities, new military technologies, different enemies, different economic competitors, and different pirates.  

The principles endure.  The policies adapt.  

That is not a slogan.  It is the actual record of 250 years, from the shores of Tripoli to the Strait of Hormuz.

Two hundred fifty years young

On this Fourth of July, it is worth remembering who the Founders actually were.

They were not only warriors.  They were farmers who read Cicero, soldiers who quoted Scripture, and statesmen who built a navy while writing treatises on liberty.  

They were fine intellectuals with deep convictions, most of them anchored in the Christian faith of families that had crossed an ocean to worship freely.  

They pledged everything they had to a principle, and then, remarkably, they spent the rest of their lives proving the principle could govern.

They left their descendants a foreign policy worthy of the republic they founded: trade with all nations, entangling alliances with none, strength sufficient to command respect, war only when justice and interest demand it, and a jealous, unsleeping defense of American sovereignty.

Two hundred fifty years later, the compass they built still points true.

The ships are steel now.  Some of them fly through space.  The pirates have missiles and enrichment facilities.  

But the great rule of conduct remains exactly where Washington left it, and the nation that follows it remains what the Founders prayed it would be: free, prosperous, sovereign, and, by the grace of God, still standing.

The “global glut” argument is misreading the data – it’s interpreting a localised bottleneck and mistaking it for a global condition, then reinforcing itself by pointing back at the price fall as proof. 

Two finite mechanisms are pushing prices down right now: SPR and Chinese reserve drawdowns, and the release of the Gulf’s wartime backlog. Both are one-off, and both are draining. 

The Gulf backlog is exiting into a market where China, the barrels’ main buyer, has temporarily stepped back. UAE and Iraq heavily discounted the trapped oil that exited to clear the backlog. China’s teapots opportunistically bought it cheap. Iran didn’t match it, focusing instead on long-term arrangements rather than a discount that isn’t lasting – its oil was placed in floating storage in the meantime. 

West African crude explains the same story from a different angle. Asian refiners paid a premium for it during the shortage, when Gulf supply was cut off and any barrel would do. That premium has gone now that Gulf crude is back and cheap, which is why a Congo cargo is currently unsold at a record $14 discount to Brent.

If the glut were global, prices would be falling everywhere at roughly the same rate, but they aren’t. The WTI-Brent spread has remained in its normal $2-8, while other crude such as Oman and Djeno is heavily discounted against those global benchmarks. That divergence, not the general price fall, is the critical piece of evidence. A global surplus would push every grade down at a similar rate, but this is pushing specific grades down much faster than others.

OPEC production in June was still 28% below February levels, and US inventories are near a 40-year low. That combination is what you’d expect from a one-off backlog and reserve drawdown pushing the marginal price down worldwide while the underlying position stays tight, not from a genuine global surplus.
